overview.body: All semester you've been building Deep in the Heart: A Lone Star Journey with dictionaries, functions, and files. The game works — but city data is scattered. CITY_DISTANCES lives in one place, CITY_INFO in another, and the functions that use them are somewhere else entirely. This week, that changes. You'll build a City class that bundles a city's name, type, tagline, and connections into one object. Then you'll refactor your Module 4 game to use City objects instead of those two dictionaries — same game, better structure. You'll also add one new function to function_library.py that gives the travel loop the pacing it has been missing all semester.

mentorQuote.quote: Two files. Two jobs. city.py defines the class — nothing else. lab-5-1.py imports it and runs the full game. When city data and behavior live in one object, you stop chasing dictionaries across your codebase. That's the whole point of OOP.

objectives: Define a City class with an __init__ constructor and private attributes
Write getter methods, connection methods, status methods, and __str__
Add enter_to_continue() to function_library.py as the library's final function
Refactor lab-4-3.py into lab-5-1.py using City objects in place of the two dictionaries
See that OOP makes the game more readable without changing how it plays
overview.textbookReference: Starting Out with Python, 6th Edition by Tony Gaddis — Chapter 10: Classes and Object-Oriented Programming
programRequirements: city.py defines a class named City with private attributes
City has status methods: is_win_city() and is_dead_end() — is_dead_end() handles both DEAD_END and WRONG_WAY
City has a get_arrival_message() method and a display_destinations() method
lab-5-1.py imports City and uses City objects in place of the distances and city_info dictionaries
overview.filenameNote: Submit city.py (City class), lab-5-1.py (refactored game), and function_library.py (updated with enter_to_continue) this week.
overview.additionalFilesNote: All three files must be present. CodeGrade tests that lab-5-1.py can import City from city.py and that function_library has enter_to_continue.

rows.task: Add enter_to_continue() and refactor the game in lab-5-1.py

timeGuide.strategy: Build and test the City class first (Checkpoints 1-4) before touching the game. Once all City methods work, the refactor in Checkpoint 5 is mostly find-and-replace: swap dictionary lookups for method calls. Take it one substitution at a time.

beforeYouBegin.intro: Here is the structure of what you are building this week. city.py contains only the class definition. lab-5-1.py is your lab-4-3.py refactored to use City objects. function_library.py gets one new function. Each checkpoint adds one section to the class, then Checkpoint 5 wires it all together.
beforeYouBegin.architecture:
# ---- city.py ----
class City:

    # --- Constructor ---   [CP1]
    __init__(self, name, city_type, tagline)
        self.__name          = name
        self.__city_type     = city_type
        self.__tagline       = tagline
        self.__connections   = {}     (empty dict to start)

    # --- Getter Methods ---   [CP2]
    get_name(self)                    returns self.__name
    get_type(self)                    returns self.__city_type
    get_tagline(self)                 returns self.__tagline

    # --- Connection Methods ---   [CP3]
    add_connection(self, city, dist)  stores in self.__connections
    get_distance_to(self, city)       returns distance or -1
    get_destination_names(self)       returns list of connected names

    # --- Status Methods ---   [CP4]
    is_win_city(self)                 True if type == 'WIN'
    is_dead_end(self)                 True if type == 'DEAD_END' or 'WRONG_WAY'
    get_arrival_message(self)         returns message string based on city type
    display_destinations(self)        prints numbered menu of connections
    __str__(self)                     returns 'San Antonio (START)'


# ---- function_library.py ----   [CP5]
enter_to_continue(prompt)            [NEW] pauses game; waits for Enter


# ---- lab-5-1.py ----   [CP5]
from city import City
import function_library

load_city_distances(filename)         unchanged from lab-4-3.py
load_city_info(filename)              unchanged from lab-4-3.py
load_hazards(filename)                unchanged from lab-4-3.py
load_trivia(filename)                 unchanged from lab-4-3.py

build_cities(distances, city_info)    [NEW] converts dicts to City objects
get_destination_by_number(city, n)    [REPLACED] now takes City object
trivia_gate(dest, trivia, ...)        [REPLACED] uses city.get_distance_to()
handle_hazard(hazards, unique)        unchanged
travel_to(dest, dist, ...)            [REPLACED] uses enter_to_continue()

main():
    load all data files               unchanged
    cities = build_cities(...)        [NEW]
    game loop uses city objects       [REPLACED] method calls replace dict lookups

if __name__ == '__main__': -> main()

checkpoints.title: File Setup & Constructor
checkpoints.subtitle: Create city.py and define the City class with its __init__ constructor.

content.body: The foundation of your City class: a properly documented file, the class definition, and an __init__ method that initializes four private attributes.

This is the moment everything changes. Instead of juggling separate dictionaries for city names, distances, and info, a City object owns its own data. Every game, app, and system you'll build professionally uses objects to bundle related data and behavior together. Master this pattern and you're thinking like a software engineer.

content.role: Director of Technology, Bat City Collective
content.quote: In Module 4, you had CITY_INFO dictionaries and CITY_DISTANCES dictionaries and functions scattered everywhere. Now? A City object knows its own data. The constructor is the birth certificate — it says exactly what a City needs to exist.

references.description: You typed a CellPhone class with __init__ that accepted parameters and stored them in private attributes like self.__manufact. Your City class follows the same pattern — accept name, city_type, and tagline, then store them with double-underscore prefixes.

items: Create a new file called city.py
Add the module docstring at the top:

content.code:
"""
city.py
Name: Your Name
abc123: your_abc123
Section: 001
Description: City class for the Deep in the Heart road trip game.

Chapter 10 Concepts:
- Class definition with class keyword
- __init__ constructor
- Private attributes with __ prefix
- Getter methods
- __str__ method
"""

items: Define the City class and add a class docstring:

content.code:
class City:
    """
    Represents a city in the Texas road trip game.

    Attributes (private):
        __name (str): City name
        __city_type (str): Type of city
        __tagline (str): City motto
        __connections (dict): Connected cities and distances
    """

items: Write the __init__ method that accepts name, city_type, and tagline parameters
Inside __init__, store each parameter as a private attribute: self.__name = name, self.__city_type = city_type, self.__tagline = tagline
Also initialize an empty dictionary for connections: self.__connections = {}

content.code:

san_antonio = City('San Antonio', 'START', 'Home of the Alamo')
print('City object created successfully!')
print(type(san_antonio))

Step 3: Delete the debug code before continuing.

rows: Every method needs self first — it's how Python knows which object to work with
Attributes need the self. prefix to belong to the object
Must be __init__ with DOUBLE underscores on both sides

items: city.py file with module docstring
City class with class docstring
__init__ method accepting 3 parameters
Four private attributes initialized: __name, __city_type, __tagline, __connections

checkpoints.title: Getter Methods
checkpoints.subtitle: Add methods that return the values of private attributes.

content.body: Three getter methods that provide controlled access to your City's private data. Outside code can read the city's name but can't accidentally change it.

references.description: Remember get_manufact(), get_model(), and get_retail_price()? Each just returned self.__attribute. Your getters work the same way.

items: Add a comment # --- Getter Methods --- below __init__
Define get_name(self) that returns self.__name
Define get_type(self) that returns self.__city_type
Define get_tagline(self) that returns self.__tagline
Add a docstring to each method (e.g., """Return the city's name.""")

rows: The attribute is private (double underscore) — must match exactly
Getters should return the value, not print it — the caller decides what to do with it
Without () you get the method object, not its return value

items: get_name() method with docstring
get_type() method with docstring
get_tagline() method with docstring

checkpoints.title: Connection Methods
checkpoints.subtitle: Add methods to manage city connections — adding routes and checking distances.

content.body: Three methods that let cities know about their road connections. This replaces the old CITY_DISTANCES dictionary — now each city manages its own connections!

items: Add a comment # --- Connection Methods ---
Define add_connection(self, city_name, distance) that stores the connection:
Define get_distance_to(self, city_name) that returns the distance, or -1 if not connected:
Define get_destination_names(self) that returns a list of connected city names:

Returns a dict_keys object, not a list — wrap with list()
Forgetting to add docstrings to methods
CodeGrade checks for documentation — every method needs a docstring

items: add_connection() method with docstring
get_distance_to() returning distance or -1, with docstring
get_destination_names() returning a list, with docstring

content.body: Methods that let you ask a city about itself: Is this a win city? A dead end? What message should display when the player arrives? Plus display_destinations() and the special __str__ method Python calls automatically when you print() a City object.

references.description: You typed a __str__ method that returned a formatted string. Python automatically calls this when you print() an object. Your City's __str__ should return something like 'San Antonio (START)'.

items: Add a comment # --- Status Methods ---
Define is_win_city(self) that returns True if self.__city_type == 'WIN' -- add a docstring
Define is_dead_end(self) that returns True if city type is 'DEAD_END' or 'WRONG_WAY' -- use self.__city_type in ['DEAD_END', 'WRONG_WAY']
Define get_arrival_message(self) that returns a different string for each city type: a beach celebration for WIN, a warning for DEAD_END and WRONG_WAY, and a standard welcome for everything else
Add a comment # --- Display Methods ---
Define display_destinations(self) that prints a numbered menu of connections
Define __str__(self) that returns a string like 'San Antonio (START)'

content.code:
def display_destinations(self):
    """Display available destinations from this city."""
    print(f'From {self.__name}, you can travel to:')
    option_num = 1
    for dest_name, distance in self.__connections.items():
        print(f'  {option_num}. {dest_name} - {distance} miles')
        option_num = option_num + 1

items: Define __str__(self) that returns a string like 'San Antonio (START)':

content.code:
# DEBUG - DELETE BEFORE CHECKPOINT 5
corpus = City('Corpus Christi', 'WIN', 'Sparkling City by the Sea')
laredo = City('Laredo', 'DEAD_END', 'Gateway to Mexico')
el_paso = City('El Paso', 'WRONG_WAY', 'Closer to California')
san_antonio = City('San Antonio', 'START', 'Home of the Alamo')

san_antonio.add_connection('Houston', 200)
san_antonio.add_connection('Corpus Christi', 150)

print('Testing __str__:')

print(f'  {corpus}')
print(f'  {laredo}')
print()
print('Testing status methods:')
print(f'  Corpus is_win_city: {corpus.is_win_city()}')
print(f'  Laredo is_dead_end: {laredo.is_dead_end()}')
print(f'  El Paso is_dead_end: {el_paso.is_dead_end()}')
print()
print('Testing get_arrival_message:')
print(f'  San Antonio: {san_antonio.get_arrival_message()}')
print(f'  Corpus Christi: {corpus.get_arrival_message()}')
print(f'  Laredo: {laredo.get_arrival_message()}')
print(f'  El Paso: {el_paso.get_arrival_message()}')
print()
print('Testing display_destinations:')
san_antonio.display_destinations()

checkpoints.title: Add enter_to_continue() and Refactor the Game
checkpoints.subtitle: Add the final function to function_library.py, then refactor lab-4-3.py into lab-5-1.py using City objects.

content.body: Two things this checkpoint. First, add enter_to_continue(prompt) to function_library.py -- the library's final function. Then copy lab-4-3.py to lab-5-1.py and make targeted substitutions: replace distances and city_info dictionary lookups with City object method calls. The trivia, hazards, D20 travel loop, and file loading all stay exactly as they were.

This is the whole OOP payoff. The game plays identically to lab-4-3.py -- but instead of two separate dictionaries you have to keep in sync, each city object owns its own data. Adding a new city means creating one object. Checking its type means calling a method. The code reads like what it does.

content.quote: The function library you started in Module 2 just got its final entry. flip_coin(), roll_d20(), get_valid_int(), clean_input() -- and now enter_to_continue(). Four modules. One function at a time. That's how real libraries grow.

items: Open function_library.py and add this function at the bottom, under a new # GAME FLOW UTILITIES (Module 5.1) comment:

content.code:
def enter_to_continue(prompt):
    """
    Pause the game and wait for the player to press Enter.

    Args:
        prompt (str): The message to display before waiting.
    """
    input(f'  {prompt}')

items: Copy lab-4-3.py to a new file named lab-5-1.py
Update the header docstring: change the lab number and description
Add from city import City near the top alongside existing imports

Add a build_cities(distances, city_info) function: loop over city_info and create a City object for each entry, then loop over distances and call city.add_connection() for each route. Return the resulting dictionary.
In main(), call cities = build_cities(distances, city_info) right after loading the data files
Update the game loop using the substitution table below -- change one line at a time and test after each change
In the travel_to() function, add function_library.enter_to_continue() calls: one before the D20 roll and one after showing the result

Run python test.py from your project folder. All City tests should pass. Then play the game end-to-end -- it should behave identically to lab-4-3.py, with the Enter prompts added during travel.

You need the City object first: city = cities[current_city]
El Paso is 'WRONG_WAY' -- your method must handle both
Removing the trivia, hazards, or file loading
Nothing changes in those sections -- only city data access changes

finalChecklist.scopeReminder: Chapter 10 only. Classes, constructors, private attributes, getter methods, and special methods. No inheritance, no subclasses -- those are Chapter 11 and not part of this course.

permittedTechniques.chapters: 2-10 (all prior techniques fully permitted; Chapter 10 sections 10.1-10.3 introduced this module)
specific: Methods that check type and return a value
permittedTechniques.notPermitted: Inheritance, subclasses, method overriding, polymorphism (Chapter 11 -- not taught in this course)

warnings: Submit all three files: city.py, lab-5-1.py, and function_library.py. Missing any one will fail CodeGrade.
city.py should contain only the class definition -- no test code, no main(), no game logic.
is_dead_end() must handle both 'DEAD_END' and 'WRONG_WAY'. El Paso and Texarkana are WRONG_WAY cities -- test them explicitly.

needHelp.chapterResource: Chapter 10 BookEx Reference Guide -- step-by-step breakdowns of every textbook example; your primary reference for the City class methods
ifYouGetStuck: Re-read Programs 10-9 through 10-13 in the BookEx guide -- BankAccount and CellPhone are your class templates
Check your indentation -- every method must be indented inside the class City: block
Remember self -- every method's first parameter is self
For the refactor, change one substitution at a time and test the game after each change
Run python test.py -- it tells you exactly which method is wrong and what it returned
Come to Zoom or Discord -- OOP clicks differently for everyone; we can work through it together
needHelp.discordNote: Post in the course Discord with your checkpoint number, what you expected to happen, and what actually happened. Include the specific error message if you have one.
